title: Transnational Criminal Organization Illicit Spotter Prevention and Elimination Act
short_title: Transnational Criminal Organization Illicit Spotter Prevention and Elimination Act
stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.
plain_summary: This bill makes it a crime to help people illegally cross the border by telling them where border patrol agents are located. It also makes it illegal to break or damage things like fences and cameras that are used to protect the border. People who do these things can go to prison for up to 10 years. The bill also gives harsher punishments to people who use guns when they help others illegally enter the United States.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Transnational Criminal Organization Illicit Spotter Prevention and Elimination Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Unlawfully Hindering Immigration, Border, and Customs Controls
sections.text: This section creates new crimes and penalties for people who interfere with border security and immigration enforcement. It adds three main types of illegal activities. First, illicit spotting is when someone knowingly tells another person where law enforcement officers are located or what they are doing. This is a crime if the person shares this information to help someone else commit federal crimes related to immigration, customs, controlled substances like drugs, agriculture, money, or other border controls. The penalty is a fine and up to ten years in prison. Second, destroying United States border controls is when someone knowingly and without permission destroys, changes, or damages any fence, barrier, sensor, camera, or other physical or electronic device that the federal government uses to control the border or a port of entry. It is also illegal to build, dig, or make any structure meant to defeat, get around, or avoid these government security devices. The penalty is a fine and up to ten years in prison. If the person uses or carries a firearm while committing this crime, the penalty increases to up to twenty years in prison. Third, conspiracy and attempt means that anyone who tries to commit these crimes or plans with others to commit them will receive the same punishment as someone who actually completes the crime. This section also changes existing federal gun laws to include alien smuggling crimes. When someone uses or carries a firearm during an alien smuggling crime, they face additional penalties. Alien smuggling crimes include felonies under sections two hundred seventy four, two hundred seventy seven, or two hundred seventy eight of the Immigration and Nationality Act. The section provides definitions for key terms including what counts as brandishing a firearm, what qualifies as a crime of violence, and what constitutes a drug trafficking crime. Finally, this section makes technical changes to other parts of federal law to ensure the new provisions work properly with existing statutes. It also extends the time limit for prosecuting these crimes by adding the new section two hundred ninety five to existing time limit rules that already apply to alien smuggling under section two hundred seventy four.
